items.q: What causes global sea level rise?
items.correct: Two main factors: thermal expansion of warming seawater, and the melting of land-based ice (glaciers and ice sheets) adding water to oceans.
items.misconception: Sea level rises mainly because Arctic sea ice is melting.
items.random_wrong: Sea level rises because of underwater volcanoes pushing it up.
items.why: Floating sea ice already displaces its weight in water — its melting does not raise sea level. The contributors are LAND ice (Greenland, Antarctica) and thermal expansion.

items.q: What is the enhanced greenhouse effect?
items.correct: The strengthening of the natural greenhouse effect by human activities (mainly burning fossil fuels) which add CO2 and other gases to the atmosphere.
items.misconception: The greenhouse effect is entirely man-made and harmful.
items.random_wrong: It is the hole in the ozone layer letting in more sun.
items.why: The natural greenhouse effect is essential for life — without it Earth would be -18 degrees C. The problem is enhancement. Students also confuse it with the (separate) ozone hole.

items.q: What is the difference between qualitative and quantitative data?
items.correct: Quantitative data is numerical and measurable (traffic counts, river width); qualitative data is descriptive and non-numerical (interview quotes, photo descriptions, field sketches).
items.misconception: Quantitative data is always more scientific and reliable than qualitative.
items.random_wrong: Qualitative data means high-quality data and quantitative means a lot of data.
items.why: Both have value: numbers show patterns; words explain why. Mixed-methods studies combine them. Students wrongly assume numbers are objective and words are subjective — both can be done well or poorly.
Correct answer: Crutzen and Stoermer. “Lovelock and Margulis” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many planetary boundaries did Rockstrom et al identify in 2009?
items.why: It's 9. “12” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Roughly what percentage of global internet traffic uses submarine cables?
items.why: The real figure is 99 percent. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
